card.title: Why the Longest Flights Take Weird Routes
card.teaser: New York to Singapore in a straight line? Not quite — your plane is drawing a curve across the top of the planet.
card.coverImagePrompt: A widebody jet banking over a frozen Arctic landscape at high altitude, contrail curving against a dusky blue sky, the curvature of Earth visible on the horizon, cinematic, crisp morning light

lessons.title: The flight map lies
lessons.body: Pull up a flight from New York to Tokyo on your seatback screen. You'll notice the little plane icon isn't flying across the Pacific in a tidy horizontal line — it's arcing north over Alaska and the Bering Sea. That's not a detour. On a real, round Earth, that arc is actually the shortest path.

visual.imagePrompt: A seatback entertainment screen showing a flight path arcing far north over Alaska between New York and Tokyo, warm cabin lighting

lessons.title: Great circles, not straight lines
lessons.body: The shortest distance between any two points on a sphere is called a great circle — a slice that cuts the globe in half. On a flat map (which stretches the poles into a horizontal bar), a great circle looks curved. On a real globe, it's as straight as you can get. Pilots fly the globe, not the poster.

keyTerms.definition: The shortest path between two points on a sphere, lying on a circle that divides the sphere in half
The common flat world map that distorts distances, especially near the poles

visual.imagePrompt: A globe with a string pulled taut between New York and Tokyo, clearly curving over the Arctic, soft studio lighting

lessons.title: Then the jet stream gets involved
lessons.body: Great circles are the geometry. But the atmosphere has its own opinions. A river of wind called the jet stream rips west-to-east across the northern mid-latitudes at 100 to 250 mph. Eastbound flights happily ride it; westbound flights have to duck under or around it. That's why a flight from London to New York often takes an hour longer than the return.

keyTerms.definition: A fast, narrow air current flowing from west to east, typically at cruising altitude

visual.imagePrompt: A satellite-style visualization of the North Atlantic jet stream as glowing orange ribbons over a dark blue Earth at night

lessons.title: The record-breaker
lessons.body: Singapore Airlines flight SQ23 from Newark to Singapore runs around 9,500 miles and takes up to 19 hours. Its route doesn't go straight east across the Atlantic — it often swings north over Greenland, then south across Central Asia, chasing tailwinds and avoiding restricted airspace. Every mile costs fuel, so airlines re-plan the route daily based on that morning's winds.
lessons.funFact: Flight plans for ultra-long-haul routes can vary by 300+ miles day to day, purely due to wind.

visual.imagePrompt: A long-haul aircraft at a rainy gate at dusk with 'SINGAPORE' on the departures board, moody reflections on wet tarmac

lessons.title: Geometry plus weather plus politics
lessons.body: A flight path is never really 'drawn' — it's negotiated. Geometry says go over the pole. Weather says hug the jet stream. Politics says avoid certain airspace. The curve on your screen is the daily compromise between all three.

quiz.question: Why do long flights appear to take a curved route on a flat map?
options: Because great circles look curved on flat projections
Because planes can't fly in straight lines
quiz.explanation: The shortest path on a sphere is a great circle. Flat maps distort this into a curve, especially near the poles.

quiz.question: Why is a London to New York flight often longer than New York to London?
options: The westbound flight fights a headwind from the jet stream
quiz.explanation: The jet stream flows west-to-east, so flying west means fighting it while flying east means riding it.

quiz.question: How often is an ultra-long-haul flight plan typically recalculated?
quiz.explanation: Airlines re-plan the exact route every day to chase tailwinds, avoid headwinds, and save fuel.

sources.title: Why Do Flight Paths Look Curved on a Map?
Great Circle Routes and Jet Stream Effects on Commercial Aviation
